critique.summary: The article discusses the evolution of spam emails, highlighting their increasingly sophisticated and attractive design, which makes them harder to detect and more convincing. It also explores the rise of 'vibe coding' in scams and the challenges it presents for online security.
mainPoints: Spam emails have become more visually appealing and better designed, making them harder to identify as scams.
Vibe coding refers to the use of aesthetic design elements to make scam emails appear more trustworthy.
Advances in AI and no-code tools have lowered the technical barriers for creating malicious schemes, increasing the threat of scams.
There are still some indicators, like email obfuscation and the use of email aliases, to help identify scam emails.
critique.truthfulness: The article accurately describes the trend of increasingly sophisticated spam and scam emails, supported by references to reports from security companies like Guard.io and general knowledge about AI-enabled scams. However, some claims about the specific techniques and the extent of 'vibe coding' are anecdotal and lack detailed empirical evidence, which slightly limits its overall credibility.
considerations: The article does not provide specific data or studies quantifying the increase in scam success rates due to improved email design.
It does not address potential countermeasures or technological solutions beyond basic email filtering techniques.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the evolving nature of spam emails, particularly with the use of AI and 'vibe coding' to make them more convincing. Commenters note that while the problem is not new, AI has increased the sophistication and scale of phishing and spam, raising concerns about email security and user awareness.

comments.author: Ucalegon
comments.text:
Leaders in the email security space have been seeing this for a while now [0], this is not new. The problem is the means to protect consumer mailboxes outside of Gmail, isn't cost effective since most people do not actually pay for their consumer mailbox and the impacts of compromised accounts do not actually impact the providers. It is going to be interesting to see how this plays out in the consumer space as the complexity of the problem continues to grow while the technology used to stop it stays in the early-2010s.

[0] https://siliconangle.com/2023/12/19/new-report-warns-rise-ai...
comments.analysis: This comment provides a well-informed perspective on the ongoing challenge of email security, highlighting economic and technical factors that limit protection outside major providers.

comments.author: hollow-moe
comments.text: They don't even need to actually vibecode the emails. Some scam reached my gmail inbox for the french railway company advantage card at a "too low to believe" price. They just downloaded an original email, replaced content urls to their own host and all links to their scam page. Yes, all links even the socials lol. There's one link that was removed instead of replaced (but the text was still there): the unsubscribe notice. I didn't check the page but the email was well done since it just was an edited official one and if the page was equally made I'm sure at least some people got scammed there.
comments.analysis: The user shares a concrete example of a scam email using original content with malicious links, illustrating the practical reality of sophisticated phishing tactics.

comments.author: sudo_cowsay
comments.text:
But is this something new? Wasn't using AI for scamming around for a long time?

Scammers started using LLMs to write fishing emails, then scammers started generating images, then they started using AI to vibe code it. Its just a natural progression.

From https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=47435156, we can know that India has a ~70% positive view on AI. While scammers likely didn't fill out the survey, it shows the general view on AI from where most scammers work from and live.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately traces the evolution of AI use in scams, emphasizing that AI-driven spam is a natural progression rather than a sudden phenomenon.

comments.author: userbinator
comments.text: The (now possibly vibe-coded) email clients hiding link destinations and the real senders' addresses as well as making it very hard to see the actual message content including all headers don't help either. Scammers might get the visible body content very convincing, but one look at the Received: and From: headers is still a reliable way to discern.
comments.analysis: The comment insightfully points out how email client design choices can hinder users' ability to detect phishing, adding to the problem beyond just spam sophistication.

comments.author: vancroft
comments.text: Email clients should just strip out hyperlinks. You link in the email? Write it directly, then people can copy/paste it. It wouldn't stop all phishing, but it would be a start to increase people's awareness of shady links.
comments.analysis: This suggestion offers a practical mitigation strategy to reduce phishing risks by increasing user awareness, reflecting a constructive approach to the issue.

comments.author: eightman
comments.text: The use case for AI is, was and always will be spam.
comments.analysis: This comment is overly pessimistic and dismissive, ignoring the broad range of AI applications beyond spam, thus reducing its accuracy and nuance.

comments.author: mememememememo
comments.text:
It is better to use the term phishing for spam that is attempting to comprimise your security, over just trying to sell something.

LLMs are interesting for phishing as they allow personalisation. Spam is no longer, well exactly the Monty Python meaning.
comments.analysis: The distinction between phishing and spam is valid and important, and the comment correctly highlights how LLMs enhance phishing through personalization.

comments.author: imiric
comments.text:
This is hardly new, and it goes far beyond spam emails. Most of the content produced and consumed on the internet is now done by machines. A human may or may not benefit from directing a machine to do this, and the ways they do are often highly opaque, with several layers of indirection. It doesn't take a genius to see that this is ushering in a new era of scams and spam.

"AI" companies are responsible for this mess. They should be held accountable for digging us out of it.
comments.analysis: While the comment raises a broader societal concern about AI-generated content and accountability, its sweeping blame on AI companies lacks nuance and specific evidence.

comments.author: autoexec
comments.text: The mail I care about doesn't look like ad copy. It's usually plain-text or at least reads fine when displayed that way. It comes from people I know and/or care about. Attached images don't display by default. Remotely hosted anything doesn't even get requested. Fancier looking spam is just going to be easier to spot.
comments.analysis: This comment offers a practical user perspective on distinguishing legitimate emails from spam, emphasizing simplicity as a trust factor.

comments.author: shusaku
comments.text: For years I’ve read people claim that the reason spam emails were low quality was to filter for idiots. If the spammers are now reaching for coding agents to clean up the presentation, it seems that theory was bunk.
comments.analysis: The comment critically examines a common theory about spam quality, noting that improved spam presentation challenges previous assumptions.
